old_claim.note: Maritime Law Codes provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The port-linked tax account records fees in drachmas and directs sale proceeds and prior cash advances through public financial administration.

source_shape.source_locator: AIO RO 26 translated lines 27-29 require fees of twenty drachmas per portion; lines 51-61 discuss sale proceeds, advance payments, and public monies.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The inscription records monetary fees and public financial administration alongside grain-tax collection.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old maritime-law edge was too broad; the source demonstrates that monetary accounting is the concrete financial component in this scoped port-tax record.

ontology_before: Port Customs Ledgers depended directly on Maritime Law Codes, implying general maritime legal doctrine was the key prerequisite for the records.
ontology_after: Port Tax Accounts keeps the scope on written fiscal/accounting records, with currency supporting fees, proceeds, and cash advances in the public account.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: port_customs_ledgers now depends on currency and no longer directly depends on maritime_law_codes.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to maritime insurance or sea-loan law rather than tax/account records.
A source shows the scoped records were non-monetary cargo manifests with no fees, proceeds, or cash-accounting component.
A future maritime_customs_law node is added and better captures the legal framing without replacing currency.
short_reason: Currency is the direct accounting substrate for fees and proceeds; maritime law was an overbroad dependency.
